content:
First, note that  
\(S = 9 + 9^2 + \cdots + 9^{2010} = 9\;\frac{9^{2010}-1}{9-1} = \frac{9^{2011}-9}{8}\).  
We check small primes \(p\) in turn:

1. \(p=2\):  
   Mod 2, each \(9^k\equiv1\), so \(S\equiv2010\cdot1\equiv0\pmod2\).  
2. \(p=3\):  
   Every term \(9^k\) is a multiple of 9, so \(S\equiv0\pmod3\).  
3. \(p=5\):  
   \(9\equiv4\pmod5\), and \(4^1,4^2,4^3,4^4,\dots\) mod 5 cycles \(4,1,4,1,\dots\).  
   Since 2010 is even, the sum of each \((4+1)\)–pair is 0 mod 5, so \(S\equiv0\pmod5\).  
4. \(p=7\):  
   \(9\equiv2\pmod7\), so  
   \(S\equiv\sum_{i=1}^{2010}2^i \equiv\frac{2^{2011}-2}{2-1}\pmod7  
           =2^{2011}-2\).  
   The order of 2 mod 7 is 3, and \(2011\equiv1\pmod3\), so \(2^{2011}\equiv2\).  
   Hence \(S\equiv2-2=0\pmod7\).  
5. \(p=11\):  
   \(9^{2011}\equiv9^{2011\bmod10}\equiv9^1=9\pmod{11}\) (by Fermat),  
   so \(\;9^{2011}-9\equiv0\pmod{11}\implies S\equiv0\pmod{11}.\)  
6. \(p=13\):  
   Similarly \(9^{2011}\equiv9^{2011\bmod12}\equiv9^7\equiv9\pmod{13}\)  
   (since \(9^3\equiv1\pmod{13}\)), so \(S\equiv0\pmod{13}\).  
7. \(p=17\):  
   We have  
   \(S=\frac{9^{2011}-9}{8}\pmod{17}\).  
   First, \(9^{2011}\equiv9^{2011\bmod16}\equiv9^{11}\pmod{17}\).  
   Compute \(9^2\equiv13\), \(9^4\equiv13^2\equiv16\), \(9^8\equiv16^2\equiv1\),  
   so \(9^{11}=9^8\cdot9^3\equiv1\cdot(9\cdot9^2)\equiv9\cdot13\equiv15\pmod{17}\).  
   Thus \(9^{2011}-9\equiv15-9=6\).  Since \(8^{-1}\equiv15\pmod{17}\),  
   \(S\equiv15\cdot6=90\equiv5\pmod{17}\neq0\).  

Hence the smallest prime *not* dividing \(S\) is  
\(\boxed{17}\).

message.content:
First, note that  
\(S = 9 + 9^2 + \cdots + 9^{2010} = 9\;\frac{9^{2010}-1}{9-1} = \frac{9^{2011}-9}{8}\).  
We check small primes \(p\) in turn:

1. \(p=2\):  
   Mod 2, each \(9^k\equiv1\), so \(S\equiv2010\cdot1\equiv0\pmod2\).  
2. \(p=3\):  
   Every term \(9^k\) is a multiple of 9, so \(S\equiv0\pmod3\).  
3. \(p=5\):  
   \(9\equiv4\pmod5\), and \(4^1,4^2,4^3,4^4,\dots\) mod 5 cycles \(4,1,4,1,\dots\).  
   Since 2010 is even, the sum of each \((4+1)\)–pair is 0 mod 5, so \(S\equiv0\pmod5\).  
4. \(p=7\):  
   \(9\equiv2\pmod7\), so  
   \(S\equiv\sum_{i=1}^{2010}2^i \equiv\frac{2^{2011}-2}{2-1}\pmod7  
           =2^{2011}-2\).  
   The order of 2 mod 7 is 3, and \(2011\equiv1\pmod3\), so \(2^{2011}\equiv2\).  
   Hence \(S\equiv2-2=0\pmod7\).  
5. \(p=11\):  
   \(9^{2011}\equiv9^{2011\bmod10}\equiv9^1=9\pmod{11}\) (by Fermat),  
   so \(\;9^{2011}-9\equiv0\pmod{11}\implies S\equiv0\pmod{11}.\)  
6. \(p=13\):  
   Similarly \(9^{2011}\equiv9^{2011\bmod12}\equiv9^7\equiv9\pmod{13}\)  
   (since \(9^3\equiv1\pmod{13}\)), so \(S\equiv0\pmod{13}\).  
7. \(p=17\):  
   We have  
   \(S=\frac{9^{2011}-9}{8}\pmod{17}\).  
   First, \(9^{2011}\equiv9^{2011\bmod16}\equiv9^{11}\pmod{17}\).  
   Compute \(9^2\equiv13\), \(9^4\equiv13^2\equiv16\), \(9^8\equiv16^2\equiv1\),  
   so \(9^{11}=9^8\cdot9^3\equiv1\cdot(9\cdot9^2)\equiv9\cdot13\equiv15\pmod{17}\).  
   Thus \(9^{2011}-9\equiv15-9=6\).  Since \(8^{-1}\equiv15\pmod{17}\),  
   \(S\equiv15\cdot6=90\equiv5\pmod{17}\neq0\).  

Hence the smallest prime *not* dividing \(S\) is  
\(\boxed{17}\).
